one_line_takeaway: PromptLocate is the first prompt-injection localizer: a three-step segment-split, tailored-oracle group search, and contextual-inconsistency pipeline that pinpoints injected instruction and data spans with 0.93-0.99 RL/ES, enabling forensic attribution and near-full clean-data recovery even when the underlying detector misses most contaminated segments.
key_finding: PromptLocate localizes injected prompts accurately and far better than attribution baselines. On OpenPromptInjection it achieves 0.93-0.99 RL and ES and 0.95-1.00 Precision/Recall across all seven attacks, versus attribution baselines that mostly score 0.2-0.6 RL/ES (their best case, on NeuralExec, reaches ~0.74-0.78). Removing localized prompts drops ASV from ASV-B ~0.28-0.50 down to ASV-A ~0.06-0.08. It succeeds despite an imperfect oracle: at the segment level DataSentinel misses 81% of contaminated segments and even the tailored oracle misses 63%, yet the group-based search plus Step III recover accuracy. It generalizes (validation used only sentiment/Combined Attack; fine-tuning data distributionally different) and is efficient (~11s per sample on RTX A5000, 5.8s on H100, sub-linear in prompt length via binary search). It stays effective under all 8 adaptive attacks (RL 0.86-0.96). Two applications: forensic analysis identifies malicious reviews at FPR/FNR 0.01/0.02 (Combined) and 0.00/0.04 (NeuralExec) vs 0.60/0.69 for standard DataSentinel individual-segment; data recovery restores 85-100% of clean-task performance (PNA-T) in most settings.
core_contribution: PromptLocate, presented as the first method for localizing (not merely detecting or preventing) an injected prompt within a contaminated LLM input sample, for the purposes of post-attack forensic analysis and clean-data recovery. Whereas prior prompt-injection defenses only prevent or flag whole-sample contamination, PromptLocate pinpoints which spans of the data are the injected prompt. It works in three steps: (I) split the contaminated data into semantically coherent segments using cosine similarity between consecutive word embeddings (boundary placed when similarity falls below a validation-chosen threshold tau=0); (II) identify segments contaminated by injected INSTRUCTIONS using an 'oracle' -- a version of DataSentinel tailored to classify segments rather than whole samples -- together with a multi-round segment-group binary search that classifies the concatenation S[1:i] rather than isolated segments; (III) pinpoint segments contaminated by injected DATA (which detectors miss because they key on instructions) using a Contextual Inconsistency Score computed from an LLM's (GPT-2) drop in probability of generating the following segments, exploiting the heuristics that injected data follows injected instructions and is contextually inconsistent with clean target data. Steps II and III are alternated. The tailored-oracle idea, group-based search, and Step III are the authors' own; DataSentinel, KAD, the attacks, and the attribution baselines are cited prior work.
threat_model: The paper states a threat model explicitly (Section 3.2). Attacker: a strong adversary with white-box access to the backend LLM, the target instruction, and the target data, and -- to cover adaptive attacks -- knowledge of the localization method itself; the attacker controls only a portion of the untrusted target data (e.g., a subset of product reviews or web comments), since localization is moot if the whole input is attacker-controlled. Attacker goal: embed injected prompt(s) so the backend LLM performs an injected task instead of the target task, possibly by embedding (not just appending) prompts, inserting multiple prompts, or crafting prompts to evade each localization step. Defender goal: localize the injected prompt(s); the defender does NOT know how many injected prompts exist, nor the attack type or injected task, and (to support closed-source deployments) has NO access to the backend LLM, only a small open-weight detection LLM used inside the oracle.
limitations: Authors' acknowledged limitations: (1) When the target task and injected task are the same type, an adaptive attack degenerates into an adversarial-example / misinformation attack that manipulates only data without added instructions -- notoriously hard to detect, so PromptLocate (or any method) has limited effectiveness. (2) PromptLocate depends on a tailored detector as oracle, so any attack able to evade the detector also evades localization. (3) Data recovery is imperfect; residual injected traces may still mislead the LLM, forcing a denial-of-service vs residual-risk trade-off for service providers. (4) LLM 'sanitization' is fundamentally harder than classic SQL/XSS injection defenses because LLMs lack a crisp instruction/data boundary. Reviewer-observed limitations: recall is weaker on AgentDojo Slack (0.73), lowering recovery there to ~0.71 (~80% of PNA-T); ASV-A does not reach zero even under exact removal (~0.06 baseline noise from same-type classification coincidences); CIS relies on GPT-2, a weak probability model; validation threshold and oracle are both tuned using Combined Attack, which could favor that regime.

datasets_used: OpenPromptInjection benchmark (7 tasks; 49 target-injected combinations; 4,900 contaminated samples per attack)
AgentDojo benchmark (Banking, Travel, Slack, Workspace environments; GPT-4o backend; 144/140/105/560 contaminated samples respectively)
Amazon Reviews dataset (30 products, forensic-analysis use case: 7 clean + 3 contaminated reviews per product)
TaskTracker benchmark (1,000 train / 500 test samples, discussion evaluation)
JailbreakBench (PAIR and GCG jailbreak prompts, generalization test)
DataSentinel open-source target-task fine-tuning dataset (distributionally different from benchmarks)
Validation set of 556 Combined-Attack contaminated samples for threshold selection
quantitative_results: PromptLocate on OpenPromptInjection: 0.93-0.99 RL and ES, 0.95-1.00 Precision/Recall across all 7 attacks (e.g., Naive Attack RL 0.97 / ES 0.98)
ASV after removal (ASV-A) for PromptLocate ~0.06-0.08 vs ASV-B ~0.28-0.50 across attacks
Attribution baselines RL/ES mostly 0.2-0.6 (best case NeuralExec ~0.74-0.78; e.g., SFA-H Naive RL 0.26)
AgentDojo PromptLocate: Banking RL 0.96/ES 0.97, Travel 0.87/0.89, Slack 0.81/0.84 (Recall 0.73), Workspace 0.86/0.91; ASV-A 0.00-0.06
Oracle imperfection: standard DataSentinel misses 81% of contaminated segments at segment level; tailored oracle still misses 63%
Adaptive attacks (Combined-Attack payload): RL 0.86-0.96, ES 0.84-0.93; ASV-A 0.07-0.09 (e.g., Swap RL 0.87/ES 0.85, Concat 0.86/0.84)
Forensic analysis FPR/FNR: Tailored-DS+Segment-group 0.01/0.02 (Combined), 0.00/0.04 (NeuralExec) vs DS+Individual 0.60/0.69 and 0.19/0.82
Data recovery: recovered data retains 85-100% of PNA-T in most cases (e.g., sentiment PNA-T 0.94 / PA-T 0.40 / PR 0.88; summarization 0.31/0.07/0.31); Slack lower at ~0.71 vs PNA-T 0.90

Ablations: Step I embedding vs sentence vs word segmentation (adaptive Combined) RL 0.92 vs 0.83 vs 0.81; Step II tailored-DS+segment-group RL 0.97 vs DS+individual 0.55; Step III on/off RL 0.97 vs 0.89 (existing Combined)
Runtime ~11s avg on RTX A5000, 5.8s on H100; sub-linear scaling (76-word sample 10.09s vs 356-word 22.01s, Step II dominant)

Concurrent/prior localization adaptations underperform: Chen et al. RL/ES/ASV-A 0.53/0.53/0.13 and Abdelnabi et al. 0.76/0.72/0.10 vs PromptLocate; TaskTracker RL/ES 0.98/0.98; jailbreak PAIR 0.95/0.90, GCG 0.96/0.96
baselines_compared: Single Feature Attribution (SFA), highest-score (SFA-H) and threshold (SFA-T) variants
Standard DataSentinel (DS) as oracle with individual-segment search
Concurrent/prior localization adaptations of Chen et al. and Abdelnabi et al. (task-drift)
